title: How Living a Slow and Boring Life Made Me a Millionaire
description: In this video, a successful CEO shares his journey from homelessness in Mumbai to becoming a millionaire in New York by embracing slowness.
transcript:
I'm going to share with you five
strategies that took me from being
homeless in Mumbai to a millionaire CEO
in New York and how slowness made me
more successful more quickly. Let's dive
in. Let's start with strategy number
one. Trade shallow sprints for deep
dives. This sounds trivial, but the math
is explosive. A 1% daily improvement
doesn't lead to linear growth. According
to James Clear, it compounds to make you
nearly 38 times better in a single year.
The Japanese call this kaizen or
continuous incremental change. Wall
Street calls it compounding. Warren
Buffett said it the best. The first rule
of compounding is to never interrupt it.
So Jerry Seinfeld, the famous comedian,
used this system to become a legend. His
advice for other comedians was very
simple. Get a calendar and get a red
marker. Every day you write jokes, you
put a big red X on that day. Soon you
will build a chain. Your only job then
is to never break the chain. He didn't
say write great jokes [music] or write
17 jokes a day. What matters is the
consistency. This is the physics of
patience. Think of a stone cutter who
hammers the rock 100 [music] times
without a crack. And on the 101st blow,
the stone will split in two. It wasn't
that single strike that did it, right?
It was the cumulative force of all the
blows that came before. [music]
Breakthroughs are like that. They seem
sudden, but they are the final result of
a very slow, very long series of
accumulated actions. Here's what you can
try. First, the 9030 principle. Your
brain operates on a natural 90minute
focus cycle called ultradian cycles. So
when you're doing deep work, focus for
90 minutes and take a 30inut break.
Please take a real break. Defocus, go
for a walk, stare out the window, call
your friend, do nothing. This allows
your brain to refuel and rewire. And
second, the delegation principle. Your
time is finite. Stop wasting it on tasks
others can do better than you. I was
advising this CEO and he told me he was
running in 18 different directions. His
energy was getting fractured everywhere.
[music] So he hired a chief of staff for
one strategic reason. Outsource 30% of
what [music] he does so he can buy back
his own time. This allowed him to
reinvest his focus on areas where he was
truly irreplaceable. Now we're on to
number two. Master the long game. Peter
Jackson spent over seven years bringing
Lord of the Rings to the screen. With
four years spent just on pre-production,
he did his research. He built new
worlds, new languages, costumes, real
sets, and the result, nearly $3 billion
at the box office and 17 Academy Awards.
It's mind-blowing. But, you know, years
later, the same studio rushed him into
making the Hobbit trilogy, and the same
franchise with the same director failed
miserably because he had to run fast.
Jackson himself later admitted, "I
didn't know what the hell I was doing."
Greatness looks glamorous from the
outside, but it is built on this
mountain of slow, deliberate, and boring
repetitions. As the great drummer
Kenwood Dinard told me once, if you want
to grow fast, practice slow. If you want
to grow slow, practice fast. Here's what
to do. Find your boring competitive
advantage. If you can endure deliberate
practice longer than your competition,
mastery is inevitable. But this doesn't
mean mindlessly repeating monkey work
that AI can do better and faster. The
crucial question of our time is going to
be this. Are these mind-numbing reps
building a unique human skill or is it
[music] just repetitive monkey work?
Peter Jackson's imagination, Steve's
Jobs taste in design, [music] Kobe
Bryant's muscle memory. These are all
forms of mastery that AI won't be able
to replicate. Find yours. Strategy
number three is next. Slow down by
getting out of the productivity prison.
[music] Researchers at UC Irvine found
something pretty shocking. An average
worker takes 23 minutes to refocus
[music]
after an interruption. One quick email
glance can derail nearly half an hour of
deep work. That's how our brain works.
It's not just lost time, it's attacks.
Sophie Leroy calls it attention residue.
A portion of your cognitive bandwidth
remains stuck thinking about the
original task when you stumble through
the next one. Now pile on hundreds of
micro decisions and interruptions every
day and you wonder why you hit that
decision fatigue. Your mental fuel tank
is completely empty by then. We live in
a culture today that worships speed.
Emails at midnight, Slack pings at dawn,
calendars stuffed edge to edge. But your
brain is not a [music] computer. And
research shows that chronic multitasking
can slash your productivity output by up
to 40%. The more [music] you chase speed
and multitasking, the more you're
racking up cognitive debt that over time
will make you mentally [music] bankrupt.
Try these three things to get out of
that productivity prison. First,
recognize the con. For one week, log how
many hours went to visible activities
like emails, Slack, meetings versus
[music]
deep value creating activities like
problem solving, building something,
[music] writing, coding, learning.
Second, practice being bored. Wait in
line without your phone. Take a walk
without earbuds. Try it. And third, get
out of the zero inbox fallacy. Stop
treating [music]
inbox zero as a trophy. An empty inbox
just means that you spend your best
energy on other people's priorities,
[music]
not your own. The most productive people
don't look busy. In this post AI era,
being busy is not going to be a badge of
honor. It's the new form of
procrastination. You need a different
kind of weight training for your brain
muscles to build focus [music] and
resist this constant craving for new
stimuli. Now we're on to number four.
Your brain has two speeds and you're
stuck in the wrong gear. In a now famous
experiment, [music]
viewers were asked to count basketball
passes and midway a man in a gorilla
suit walks [music] through the frame,
pounds his chest, and strolls off. Half
the participants [music] never saw him.
That's your system one brain in action.
Fast, automatic, [music] and dangerously
blind to what it's not looking for. Our
brains have two operating systems.
[music] System one is fast and reflexive
and system two [music] is slow and
reflective. Our modern work culture
forces us into system one all day. But
almost all valuable [music] complex work
requires system two. And you know things
get even worse. Brain scans show that
system two lights up the preffrontal
cortex consuming more glucose and
[music] oxygen. So this deliberation and
deep thinking process actually burns
more energy. That's why it feels hard to
do. Engaging system 2 slows your
reaction time by a mere 200 or 400
milliseconds. [music] And remember, it
takes 300 milliseconds to blink. And
that sliver of time is where
breakthroughs are born and catastrophic
errors are [music] avoided. This isn't
just theory. It's biology. Just 8 weeks
of slow, deliberate practice like
meditation can physically thicken
[music] the gray matter in your brain,
rewiring you for excellence. And you can
also try these three methods. First,
slow your one-way doors for any
highstakes irreversible decision.
[music] Enforce a one-way door protocol
and try to sleep on it for 24 hours if
you can. This deliberately engages your
slow analytical system. Second, design
rituals to shift gears. Try two or
[music] five minutes of slow breathing.
About six breaths a minute. I do this a
lot. This primes your brain [music] for
system two. Turn your focus mode on.
This is the third idea. When it's time
to focus, I close all browser tabs.
[music] I put the phone away in this
other room. And I treat my focus mode
[music] as sacred. You can too. You have
to protect your brain from all these
false urgencies of the modern world
because sometimes the [music] faster you
move, the farther you'll fall behind.
Finally, strategy number five. You have
to know when to be slow. The Navy Seal
protocol is the great way to remember
this idea. Slow is smooth. Smooth is
fast. In combat operations where
mistakes are fatal, try to move too fast
leads to missteps that slow the entire
team. But the answer then isn't to move
really slowly either. The goal is
deliberate smooth actions that create
unstoppable momentum. Think of slowness
as a dial you can turn up or down when
[music] you need to use it. Dial up
speed for quick experiments, quick
iterations. Slow down for strategic
foundational work. Google would
experiment search ads millions of times
a day at lightning speed. But when it
came to building [music] deep AI
capabilities, I mean, they took decades.
They knew how to turn the dial. Here's
your speed dial manual. Go slow on
oneway decisions like hiring major
investments. Go slow when you are
developing deep human skills through
deliberate practice. Go slow on
foundation [music] building, on systems,
on processes, and then go fast on
reversible decisions, testing,
experimentation, routine tasks. Just go.
So, here's the final takeaway. You can
watch this video at 1.5x speed, but the
real challenge is to slow down in at
least one area of your life that
requires deep focus. And then in that
one area, trade speed for depth. Block 1
hour a week for [music] deep work. Slow
down. Observe. Improve by 1%. That is
your first step in turning slowness into
a superpower. If you like this video,
hey, subscribe to my channel [music]
and check out my next video here. I will
see you next week. Till then, I hope you
slow down to smell the roses. [music]
Thank you and I love
